nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered inner reef crests and slopes at depths of roughly 1–21 m; typically found singly or in small groups grazing detritus and algal films from hard substrate

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
open swimming space along the length of the tank
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long footprint (4–5 ft minimum) preferred over tall depth

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze algal films from live rock. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week; vitamin-rich diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion. Has been documented accepting algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and occasional meaty foods (mysis shrimp) in mixed-community feedings.

summary: A small, peaceful-with-non-tangs Indian Ocean bristletooth surgeonfish whose movable comb-like teeth make it an efficient grazer of detritus and algal films. Generally considered a good reef-safe utility tang for moderately sized systems.
careNotes:
The Squaretail Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus truncatus) is one of the smaller Ctenochaetus species, generally reaching around 15–17 cm in captivity; FishBase records a maximum of 16 cm SL in the wild. The species name truncatus refers to the distinctively squared-off caudal fin, which lacks the pointed tips typical of most other bristletooth tangs.

A tank around 300 L (~4–5 ft footprint) is generally advised. Retail sources span 70 gal (265 L, LiveAquaria/Freakincorals) at the entry-level end through 90 gal (340 L, Aquatics Unlimited) to 125 gal (470 L, ReefBay) — the cautious-larger choice reflects that bristletooth tangs are active swimmers throughout the day and benefit from a long horizontal footprint for grazing routes. Plenty of live rock with mature biofilm and algal turf is generally considered important, as the species' bristle-like teeth are specialised for sifting these substrates.

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 23–27°C — sources span 22°C (LiveAquaria's 72°F lower bound) to FishBase's modelled wild mean of ~28°C, averaged to a 23–27°C aquarium target.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week are generally advised; vitamin C supplementation is commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition this species (like most Acanthurids) is reported to develop in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet.

The species is generally considered reef-safe; isolated reports note occasional grazing on some soft corals and sessile invertebrates, but well-fed individuals tend not to pick at corals. It is best kept singly per tank — conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus and similarly shaped tangs has been reported as high — and is generally peaceable with non-tang reef fish.

As an acanthurid, this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades (the 'scalpel') used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers.

Cross-source notes: Adult size ranged from FishBase 16 cm SL through retail 6–6.5 in (15–17 cm) to ReefBay's outlier 10 in — used a 15–17 cm aquarium-realistic range. Tank minimum ranged 70 gal (265 L) to 125 gal (470 L); per the cautious-larger rule rounded to 300 L (~80 gal) reflecting the species' active grazing behaviour. Temperature spread 22–29°C across sources; averaged to 23–27°C. Native range conflict: Saltwaterfish.com listed 'Red Sea' but FishBase and other sources restrict C. truncatus to the Indian Ocean (East Africa to Sri Lanka) — the Red Sea population is the related Ctenochaetus striatus.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. FishBase classifies the species under Acanthurid spawning biology but provides no species-specific reproductive details. Like most acanthurids, pelagic broadcast spawning is presumed; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and pair-bonding constraints.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Squaretail Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (6.5 in / 16.5 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity, family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (16 cm SL), depth (1–21 m), native range (Indian Ocean — sheltered inner reef crests and slopes), preferred temperature range (24.9–29.3°C, mean ~28.2°C), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and common names (Indian gold-ring bristle-tooth, Yelloweye bristletooth).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; substituted this hobby guide. Confirmed adult size (~6 in / 15 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / 340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive temperament toward other Ctenochaetus, reef-safe, and Indian Ocean (East Africa to Sri Lanka) range.

additional.name: ReefBay Square Tail Bristletooth Tang Care Guide
additional.notes: Additional hobby guide. Suggested 10 in max (likely overstated — outlier above FishBase 16 cm SL), 125+ gal minimum (used as the upper bound for the cautious-larger tank rule), semi-aggressive with territorial behaviour toward similar species, reef compatible with caveats (may graze some soft corals).

additional.name: Freakin' Corals
additional.notes: Reef hobby retailer page. Confirmed 6.5 in max size, 70 gal minimum tank, standard reef parameters, high conspecific aggression (one per tank, may be harassed by its own species), Africa/Sri Lanka range, herbivorous diet with seaweed 3x/week.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
